sections.heading: Overview
sections.embedText: RustGuard ships as a single rustguard CLI binary built from a Cargo workspace of seven crates. The userspace daemon runs on macOS and Linux without external C dependencies. The kernel module is an optional, separate build targeting Linux 6.10 or later.
sections.content: RustGuard ships as a single `rustguard` CLI binary built from a Cargo workspace of seven crates. The userspace daemon runs on macOS and Linux without external C dependencies. The kernel module is an optional, separate build targeting Linux 6.10 or later.

sections.heading: Building from Source
sections.embedText:
Clone the repository and build all workspace crates with Cargo:

The compiled binary is placed at target/release/rustguard.

To install the binary to Cargo's local bin directory:

### Building the Kernel Module

The kernel module lives in rustguard-kmod/ and is built separately using the out-of-tree kernel build system. It requires the kernel headers for the running kernel to be installed.

Load the compiled module:
sections.content:
Clone the repository and build all workspace crates with Cargo:

```bash
git clone <repository-url>
cd rustguard
cargo build --release
```

The compiled binary is placed at `target/release/rustguard`.

To install the binary to Cargo's local bin directory:

```bash
cargo install --path rustguard-cli
```

### Building the Kernel Module

The kernel module lives in `rustguard-kmod/` and is built separately using the out-of-tree kernel build system. It requires the kernel headers for the running kernel to be installed.

```bash
cd rustguard-kmod
make KDIR=/lib/modules/$(uname -r)/build
```

Load the compiled module:

```bash
sudo insmod rustguard.ko
```

sections.heading: Examples
sections.embedText:
### End-to-End Build and Smoke Test

### Userspace Tunnel from a wg.conf File
sections.content:
### End-to-End Build and Smoke Test

```bash
# Build release binary
cargo build --release

# Verify binary works
./target/release/rustguard --help

# Generate a key pair
./target/release/rustguard genkey > /tmp/test.key
./target/release/rustguard pubkey < /tmp/test.key

# Start a server (binds enrollment, creates ~/.rustguard/state.json)
./target/release/rustguard serve --pool 10.150.0.0/24 --token mysecret &

# Check enrollment window state
./target/release/rustguard status

# Open a 60-second enrollment window
./target/release/rustguard open 60

# Clean up
./target/release/rustguard close
```

### Userspace Tunnel from a wg.conf File

```bash
# Traditional WireGuard config file mode — no enrollment required
sudo rustguard up wg0.conf
```

sections.heading: Full WireGuard Implementation and Architecture
sections.embedText: RustGuard implements the full WireGuard protocol from cryptographic primitives upward across seven Rust crates totalling 8,500+ lines of code. The project targets two runtime modes: a portable userspace daemon that runs on macOS and Linux via a TUN interface, and an out-of-tree Linux kernel module targeting kernel 6.10+ that eliminates the TUN overhead entirely.
sections.content: RustGuard implements the full WireGuard protocol from cryptographic primitives upward across seven Rust crates totalling 8,500+ lines of code. The project targets two runtime modes: a portable userspace daemon that runs on macOS and Linux via a TUN interface, and an out-of-tree Linux kernel module targeting kernel 6.10+ that eliminates the TUN overhead entirely.

sections.heading: Cryptographic and Protocol Features
sections.embedText: The implementation covers the complete WireGuard specification: X25519 key exchange, ChaCha20-Poly1305 and XChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD, HMAC-BLAKE2s, HKDF key derivation, TAI64N timestamps, the Noise_IKpsk2 handshake, sliding-window anti-replay, the cookie DoS-mitigation mechanism, pre-shared key support, and dual-stack IPv4/IPv6 routing.
sections.content: The implementation covers the complete WireGuard specification: X25519 key exchange, ChaCha20-Poly1305 and XChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD, HMAC-BLAKE2s, HKDF key derivation, TAI64N timestamps, the Noise_IKpsk2 handshake, sliding-window anti-replay, the cookie DoS-mitigation mechanism, pre-shared key support, and dual-stack IPv4/IPv6 routing.

sections.heading: Zero-Config Enrollment Layer
sections.embedText: Beyond the core protocol, RustGuard adds a zero-config enrollment layer (rustguard serve / rustguard join) that removes manual key exchange and IP planning, along with a Zigbee-style enrollment window for physical-presence security.
sections.content: Beyond the core protocol, RustGuard adds a zero-config enrollment layer (rustguard serve / rustguard join) that removes manual key exchange and IP planning, along with a Zigbee-style enrollment window for physical-presence security.

sections.heading: Crate Structure
sections.embedText:
RustGuard is organized as a Cargo workspace of seven purpose-scoped crates.

The rustguard-crypto and rustguard-core crates are no_std-capable, making them suitable for embedded and kernel contexts. All other crates depend on std.
sections.content:
RustGuard is organized as a Cargo workspace of seven purpose-scoped crates.

| Crate | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| `rustguard-crypto` | X25519, ChaCha20-Poly1305, XChaCha20-Poly1305, HMAC-BLAKE2s, HKDF, TAI64N — dual `std`/`no_std` |
| `rustguard-core` | Noise_IK handshake, transport sessions, replay window, timer state machine — dual `std`/`no_std` |
| `rustguard-tun` | macOS `utun`, Linux TUN, multi-queue TUN, AF_XDP, `io_uring`, BPF loader |
| `rustguard-daemon` | Standard `wg.conf` tunnel mode (`rustguard up`) |
| `rustguard-enroll` | Zero-config enrollment: IP pool, token-keyed exchange, Zigbee-style pairing window, persistence |
| `rustguard-cli` | CLI entry point: `up`, `serve`, `join`, `open`, `close`, `status`, `genkey`, `pubkey` |
| `rustguard-kmod` | Linux kernel module (Rust + C shim, out-of-tree, targets 6.10+) |

The `rustguard-crypto` and `rustguard-core` crates are `no_std`-capable, making them suitable for embedded and kernel contexts. All other crates depend on `std`.

sections.heading: Protocol completeness
sections.embedText:
- Noise_IKpsk2 handshake with full wire-format serialization
- Cookie mechanism (type-3 Cookie Reply messages) for rate-limiting unauthenticated initiations under load
- 2048-bit sliding-window anti-replay bitmap with split check() / update() to prevent window poisoning
- Rekey after 120 seconds or 2⁶⁰ messages; keepalive, handshake retry with jitter, session expiry

sections.heading: Operational Modes
sections.embedText:
RustGuard supports three operational modes selected by the CLI subcommand.

**Config-file mode** — compatible with existing wg.conf files:

**Enrollment server mode** — starts a WireGuard server with automatic peer enrollment:

**Client enrollment mode** — joins an enrollment server without manual key or IP configuration:

**Enrollment window control** — manages the physical-presence pairing window on a running server:

**Key generation**:
sections.content:
RustGuard supports three operational modes selected by the CLI subcommand.

**Config-file mode** — compatible with existing `wg.conf` files:

```bash
rustguard up wg0.conf
```

**Enrollment server mode** — starts a WireGuard server with automatic peer enrollment:

```bash
rustguard serve --pool 10.150.0.0/24 --token mysecret
```

**Client enrollment mode** — joins an enrollment server without manual key or IP configuration:

```bash
rustguard join 192.0.2.1:51820 --token mysecret
```

**Enrollment window control** — manages the physical-presence pairing window on a running server:

```bash
rustguard open 60    # open enrollment for 60 seconds
rustguard close      # close enrollment immediately
rustguard status     # print window state and connected peer count
```

**Key generation**:

```bash
rustguard genkey            # generate a base64-encoded private key
rustguard pubkey <privkey>  # derive the corresponding public key
```

sections.heading: Examples
sections.embedText:
The following example brings up a two-peer tunnel using config-file mode on Linux, mirroring the standard WireGuard workflow.

The enrollment workflow removes the key-exchange step entirely:
sections.content:
The following example brings up a two-peer tunnel using config-file mode on Linux, mirroring the standard WireGuard workflow.

```bash
# Generate keys for both peers
PRIV_A=$(rustguard genkey)
PUB_A=$(echo "$PRIV_A" | rustguard pubkey)

PRIV_B=$(rustguard genkey)
PUB_B=$(echo "$PRIV_B" | rustguard pubkey)

# Write peer A config
cat > peer_a.conf <<EOF
[Interface]
PrivateKey = $PRIV_A
Address = 10.0.0.1/24
ListenPort = 51820

[Peer]
PublicKey = $PUB_B
AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.2/32
EOF

# Write peer B config
cat > peer_b.conf <<EOF
[Interface]
PrivateKey = $PRIV_B
Address = 10.0.0.2/24

[Peer]
PublicKey = $PUB_A
AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.1/32
Endpoint = 127.0.0.1:51820
EOF

# Start both peers
rustguard up peer_a.conf &
rustguard up peer_b.conf &

# Verify connectivity
ping -c 3 10.0.0.2
```

The enrollment workflow removes the key-exchange step entirely:

```bash
# Terminal 1 — server
rustguard serve --pool 10.150.0.0/24 --token homelab
rustguard open 60   # open enrollment window

# Terminal 2 — client (any machine reachable to the server)
rustguard join 10.0.0.1:51820 --token homelab
# Client receives an IP from the pool and the tunnel is live
```

sections.heading: Complete Enrollment Example
sections.embedText:
The following example brings up a two-peer tunnel entirely from the command line:

After rustguard close, rustguard status output resembles:
sections.content:
The following example brings up a two-peer tunnel entirely from the command line:

```bash
# --- Server (192.168.1.10) ---
rustguard serve --pool 10.99.0.0/24 --token homelab2026 &
rustguard open 120   # open window for 2 minutes

# --- Client A (any machine) ---
rustguard join 192.168.1.10:51820 --token homelab2026
# Client A receives 10.99.0.2

# --- Client B (any machine) ---
rustguard join 192.168.1.10:51820 --token homelab2026
# Client B receives 10.99.0.3

# --- Server: close window after all peers joined ---
rustguard close
rustguard status
```

After `rustguard close`, `rustguard status` output resembles:

```
enrollment: closed
peers: 2
  10.99.0.2  last handshake: 3s ago
  10.99.0.3  last handshake: 5s ago
```

sections.heading: Operating Modes
sections.embedText:
RustGuard supports two mutually exclusive operating modes, each with its own configuration surface:

- **Standard tunnel mode** — reads a wg.conf-format file, identical to upstream WireGuard. Managed by the rustguard-daemon crate.
- **Zero-config enrollment mode** — no config files; keys and IPs are negotiated at runtime over an encrypted enrollment protocol. Managed by the rustguard-enroll crate.
sections.content:
RustGuard supports two mutually exclusive operating modes, each with its own configuration surface:

- **Standard tunnel mode** — reads a wg.conf-format file, identical to upstream WireGuard. Managed by the rustguard-daemon crate.
- **Zero-config enrollment mode** — no config files; keys and IPs are negotiated at runtime over an encrypted enrollment protocol. Managed by the rustguard-enroll crate.

sections.heading: Accessing the Modes
sections.embedText: Both modes are accessed through the same rustguard-cli binary. The mode is selected by the subcommand used.

sections.heading: Standard Tunnel Mode (`wg.conf`)
sections.embedText:
The rustguard up subcommand reads a standard WireGuard configuration file and brings up the tunnel. The file format is compatible with the upstream wg tooling.

**[Interface] fields**

**[Peer] fields**

### Bringing up a tunnel

On clean shutdown (SIGINT/SIGTERM), the daemon removes the routes it added and tears down the TUN interface.
sections.content:
The `rustguard up` subcommand reads a standard WireGuard configuration file and brings up the tunnel. The file format is compatible with the upstream `wg` tooling.

```ini
[Interface]
PrivateKey = <base64-encoded X25519 private key>
Address    = 10.0.0.1/24
ListenPort = 51820

[Peer]
PublicKey  = <base64-encoded X25519 public key>
Endpoint   = 203.0.113.5:51820
AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.2/32
```

**`[Interface]` fields**

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `PrivateKey` | Base64-encoded X25519 private key. Generate with `rustguard genkey`. |
| `Address` | IP address and prefix length assigned to the tunnel interface. Accepts both IPv4 and IPv6 CIDR notation. |
| `ListenPort` | UDP port to bind. Omit for client-only peers that initiate connections. |

**`[Peer]` fields**

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `PublicKey` | Base64-encoded X25519 public key of the remote peer. |
| `Endpoint` | `host:port` of the remote peer. Accepts IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. |
| `AllowedIPs` | Comma-separated list of CIDR ranges routed through this peer. Both IPv4 and IPv6 CIDR ranges are supported. |
| `PresharedKey` | Optional base64-encoded 32-byte pre-shared key for post-quantum resistance (PSK layer in Noise_IKpsk2). |

### Bringing up a tunnel

```bash
rustguard up wg0.conf
```

On clean shutdown (SIGINT/SIGTERM), the daemon removes the routes it added and tears down the TUN interface.

sections.heading: Key Management
sections.embedText:
RustGuard provides CLI subcommands for generating and deriving X25519 key pairs for use in wg.conf files.

Keys generated by these commands are compatible with the PrivateKey and PublicKey fields in the [Interface] and [Peer] sections of a wg.conf file.
sections.content:
RustGuard provides CLI subcommands for generating and deriving X25519 key pairs for use in `wg.conf` files.

```bash
# Generate a new private key (outputs base64 to stdout)
rustguard genkey

# Derive the public key from a private key
rustguard genkey | rustguard pubkey
```

Keys generated by these commands are compatible with the `PrivateKey` and `PublicKey` fields in the `[Interface]` and `[Peer]` sections of a `wg.conf` file.

sections.heading: Persistent State
sections.embedText:
When running in enrollment mode, the server persists its state to disk so that enrolled peers survive a restart.

**Location:** ~/.rustguard/state.json

The state file records peer public keys and their allocated IP addresses. On restart, rustguard serve reloads this file and restores all previously enrolled peers without requiring them to re-enroll. The file is written in JSON format; direct edits are unsupported and may be overwritten.
sections.content:

The state file records peer public keys and their allocated IP addresses. On restart, `rustguard serve` reloads this file and restores all previously enrolled peers without requiring them to re-enroll. The file is written in JSON format; direct edits are unsupported and may be overwritten.

sections.embedText:
### Dual-stack standard tunnel

Two peers configured for simultaneous IPv4 and IPv6 routing:

### Zero-config homelab setup

The server gets 10.150.0.1, the first client gets 10.150.0.2, the second gets 10.150.0.3, and so on.

### Generating a key pair for wg.conf
sections.content:
### Dual-stack standard tunnel

Two peers configured for simultaneous IPv4 and IPv6 routing:

```ini
# Server: wg0.conf
[Interface]
PrivateKey = <server-private-key>
Address    = 10.0.0.1/24, fd00::1/64
ListenPort = 51820

[Peer]
PublicKey  = <client-public-key>
AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.2/32, fd00::2/128
```

```ini
# Client: wg0.conf
[Interface]
PrivateKey = <client-private-key>
Address    = 10.0.0.2/24, fd00::2/64

[Peer]
PublicKey  = <server-public-key>
Endpoint   = 203.0.113.5:51820
AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0, ::/0
```

```bash
rustguard up wg0.conf
```

### Zero-config homelab setup

```bash
# On the server — start and open a 120-second enrollment window
rustguard serve --pool 10.150.0.0/24 --token homelab-secret
rustguard open 120

# On each client machine
rustguard join 192.168.1.10:51820 --token homelab-secret

# On the server — check enrolled peers
rustguard status
```

The server gets `10.150.0.1`, the first client gets `10.150.0.2`, the second gets `10.150.0.3`, and so on.

### Generating a key pair for wg.conf

```bash
PRIVATE=$(rustguard genkey)
PUBLIC=$(echo "$PRIVATE" | rustguard pubkey)
echo "PrivateKey = $PRIVATE"
echo "PublicKey  = $PUBLIC"
```

sections.heading: Overview
sections.embedText: RustGuard separates concerns across seven crates. At runtime, data travels through three distinct flows: the **tunnel loop** (encrypt/decrypt of transport packets), the **handshake flow** (Noise_IKpsk2 session establishment), and the **enrollment flow** (zero-config peer provisioning). Each flow is described below with its exact crate-level path and the security operations applied at each stage.
sections.content:
RustGuard separates concerns across seven crates. At runtime, data travels through three distinct flows: the **tunnel loop** (encrypt/decrypt of transport packets), the **handshake flow** (Noise_IKpsk2 session establishment), and the **enrollment flow** (zero-config peer provisioning). Each flow is described below with its exact crate-level path and the security operations applied at each stage.

---

sections.heading: Tunnel loop definition and steady-state operation
sections.embedText: The tunnel loop is the steady-state hot path. Once a session is established, plaintext IP packets enter via a TUN device and exit as encrypted UDP datagrams — and vice versa.
sections.content: The tunnel loop is the steady-state hot path. Once a session is established, plaintext IP packets enter via a TUN device and exit as encrypted UDP datagrams — and vice versa.

sections.heading: Outbound packet flow from TUN to UDP
sections.embedText:
### Outbound (TUN → UDP)

1. **rustguard-tun** reads a plaintext IP packet from the TUN device (Linux /dev/net/tun, macOS utun, or AF_XDP zero-copy path).
2. **rustguard-core** looks up the session by destination IP via the AllowedIPs table. It selects the current send key and atomically increments the 64-bit nonce counter.
3. **rustguard-crypto** encrypts the payload with ChaCha20-Poly1305. The nonce is derived from the counter. The 16-byte authentication tag is appended.
4. **rustguard-core** prepends the 4-byte type field and 4-byte receiver index to produce the final wire frame, which is sent over UDP.
sections.content:
### Outbound (TUN → UDP)

1. **rustguard-tun** reads a plaintext IP packet from the TUN device (Linux /dev/net/tun, macOS utun, or AF_XDP zero-copy path).
2. **rustguard-core** looks up the session by destination IP via the AllowedIPs table. It selects the current send key and atomically increments the 64-bit nonce counter.
3. **rustguard-crypto** encrypts the payload with ChaCha20-Poly1305. The nonce is derived from the counter. The 16-byte authentication tag is appended.
4. **rustguard-core** prepends the 4-byte type field and 4-byte receiver index to produce the final wire frame, which is sent over UDP.

sections.heading: Inbound packet flow from UDP to TUN
sections.embedText:
### Inbound (UDP → TUN)

1. **rustguard-core** receives a UDP datagram, extracts the receiver index, and locates the session.
2. The **replay window** check() is called before any decryption. Packets with a counter already seen — or below the window floor — are dropped immediately.
3. **rustguard-crypto** performs ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD decryption. Failure aborts; the replay window is *not* advanced.
4. Only after successful decryption does **rustguard-core** call replay window update(), advancing the 2048-bit sliding bitmap.
5. The source IP is verified against AllowedIPs. Valid packets are written to the TUN device by **rustguard-tun**.
sections.content:
### Inbound (UDP → TUN)

1. **rustguard-core** receives a UDP datagram, extracts the receiver index, and locates the session.
2. The **replay window** check() is called before any decryption. Packets with a counter already seen — or below the window floor — are dropped immediately.
3. **rustguard-crypto** performs ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD decryption. Failure aborts; the replay window is *not* advanced.
4. Only after successful decryption does **rustguard-core** call replay window update(), advancing the 2048-bit sliding bitmap.
5. The source IP is verified against AllowedIPs. Valid packets are written to the TUN device by **rustguard-tun**.

sections.heading: Handshake Overview
sections.embedText: The handshake establishes shared session keys before any transport data is exchanged, and periodically re-keys the session.
sections.content: The handshake establishes shared session keys before any transport data is exchanged, and periodically re-keys the session.

sections.heading: DoS Protection with Cookie Mechanism
sections.embedText: When the responder is under load, it rejects initiations lacking a valid MAC2. CookieState (client) stores the most recent cookie reply. CookieChecker (server) rotates secrets on a 120-second cycle and verifies MAC2. The cookie reply message (type 3) is encrypted with XChaCha20-Poly1305 using a key derived from the initiator's public key and the server's rotating secret.
sections.content: When the responder is under load, it rejects initiations lacking a valid MAC2. CookieState (client) stores the most recent cookie reply. CookieChecker (server) rotates secrets on a 120-second cycle and verifies MAC2. The cookie reply message (type 3) is encrypted with XChaCha20-Poly1305 using a key derived from the initiator's public key and the server's rotating secret.

sections.heading: Authentication Verification Order
sections.embedText: The MAC1 check always runs first — DH operations are never performed on an unauthenticated packet.
sections.content: The MAC1 check always runs first — DH operations are never performed on an unauthenticated packet.

sections.heading: Enrollment Flow Overview
sections.embedText: The enrollment flow handles zero-config peer provisioning via the serve / join commands. It is independent of the tunnel loop.
sections.content: The enrollment flow handles zero-config peer provisioning via the serve / join commands. It is independent of the tunnel loop.

sections.heading: Server Initialization
sections.embedText: **rustguard serve** initialises rustguard-enroll with a CIDR pool. The server holds .1; clients receive sequential IPs from the remainder of the pool.
sections.content: **rustguard serve** initialises rustguard-enroll with a CIDR pool. The server holds .1; clients receive sequential IPs from the remainder of the pool.

sections.heading: Enrollment Access Control
sections.embedText: Enrollment is **closed by default**. rustguard open  writes an atomic expiry timestamp over the UNIX domain control socket. rustguard close clears it immediately.
sections.content: Enrollment is **closed by default**. rustguard open  writes an atomic expiry timestamp over the UNIX domain control socket. rustguard close clears it immediately.

sections.heading: Client Enrollment and Encryption
sections.embedText: When a client calls rustguard join, rustguard-enroll derives an XChaCha20-Poly1305 key from the shared token and uses it to encrypt the key exchange. No plaintext key material crosses the wire.
sections.content: When a client calls rustguard join, rustguard-enroll derives an XChaCha20-Poly1305 key from the shared token and uses it to encrypt the key exchange. No plaintext key material crosses the wire.

sections.heading: Peer Persistence
sections.embedText: On success, the new peer (public key + assigned IP) is added to rustguard-core's peer table and persisted to ~/.rustguard/state.json so it survives daemon restarts.
sections.content: On success, the new peer (public key + assigned IP) is added to rustguard-core's peer table and persisted to ~/.rustguard/state.json so it survives daemon restarts.

sections.heading: Kernel Module Path (rustguard-kmod)
sections.embedText:
When the rustguard-kmod out-of-tree kernel module is loaded (Linux 6.10+), the TUN bottleneck is eliminated. The data path bypasses rustguard-tun entirely:

The crypto and session logic in rustguard-crypto and rustguard-core are compiled as no_std crates so they can be linked into the kernel module without the standard library.
sections.content:
When the `rustguard-kmod` out-of-tree kernel module is loaded (Linux 6.10+), the TUN bottleneck is eliminated. The data path bypasses `rustguard-tun` entirely:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Kernel netdev\nIP packets] -->|in-kernel| B[rustguard-kmod\nC shim + Rust core]
    B -->|kernel crypto API| C[ChaCha20-Poly1305\nin-kernel]
    C -->|UDP socket\nkernel space| D[Network stack]
```

The crypto and session logic in `rustguard-crypto` and `rustguard-core` are compiled as `no_std` crates so they can be linked into the kernel module without the standard library.

sections.heading: Dual execution paths
sections.embedText: RustGuard is organized into seven Rust crates that form two distinct execution paths: a userspace tunnel that runs as a daemon process managing a TUN device, and an in-kernel tunnel implemented as an out-of-tree Linux kernel module.
sections.content: RustGuard is organized into seven Rust crates that form two distinct execution paths: a userspace tunnel that runs as a daemon process managing a TUN device, and an in-kernel tunnel implemented as an out-of-tree Linux kernel module.

sections.heading: Shared cryptographic and protocol layer
sections.embedText: Both paths share the same cryptographic and protocol crates, which are written in no_std-compatible Rust to enable kernel compilation without modification.
sections.content: Both paths share the same cryptographic and protocol crates, which are written in no_std-compatible Rust to enable kernel compilation without modification.

sections.heading: Userspace modes and unified CLI
sections.embedText: The userspace path is further subdivided into a standard wg.conf-compatible mode (rustguard-daemon) and a zero-config enrollment mode (rustguard-enroll). Both modes are exposed through a unified CLI (rustguard-cli).
sections.content: The userspace path is further subdivided into a standard wg.conf-compatible mode (rustguard-daemon) and a zero-config enrollment mode (rustguard-enroll). Both modes are exposed through a unified CLI (rustguard-cli).

sections.heading: Component Diagram
sections.embedText: The diagram below shows static crate dependencies and the two runtime paths.
sections.content:
The diagram below shows static crate dependencies and the two runtime paths.

```mermaid
graph TD
    CLI["rustguard-cli\nup · serve · join · open · close · status · genkey · pubkey"]

    CLI --> DAEMON["rustguard-daemon\nwg.conf tunnel mode"]
    CLI --> ENROLL["rustguard-enroll\nZero-config enrollment · IP pool · pairing · persistence"]

    DAEMON --> CORE["rustguard-core\nNoise_IKpsk2 handshake · transport sessions\nreplay window · timers"]
    ENROLL --> CORE

    DAEMON --> TUN["rustguard-tun\nmacOS utun · Linux TUN · multi-queue\nAF_XDP · io_uring · BPF loader"]
    ENROLL --> TUN

    CORE --> CRYPTO["rustguard-crypto\nX25519 · ChaCha20-Poly1305\nHMAC-BLAKE2s · HKDF · TAI64N"]

    TUN --> OS["OS Network Stack\nTUN device fd · UDP socket"]

    KMOD["rustguard-kmod\nLinux kernel module\nRust + C shim · targets 6.10+"]
    KMOD --> CRYPTO
    KMOD --> CORE
    KMOD --> KNS["Linux Kernel Network Stack\nnetdev · sk_buff · xdp_buff"]
```

sections.heading: Userspace Mode
sections.embedText: rustguard-daemon and rustguard-enroll both run entirely in userspace. Encrypted packets are exchanged via a UDP socket; plaintext packets are injected and read from a TUN device file descriptor. rustguard-tun abstracts the platform-specific TUN API and provides multiple I/O backends (blocking read/write, multi-queue, AF_XDP zero-copy, io_uring).
sections.content: rustguard-daemon and rustguard-enroll both run entirely in userspace. Encrypted packets are exchanged via a UDP socket; plaintext packets are injected and read from a TUN device file descriptor. rustguard-tun abstracts the platform-specific TUN API and provides multiple I/O backends (blocking read/write, multi-queue, AF_XDP zero-copy, io_uring).

sections.heading: Kernel Module Mode
sections.embedText: rustguard-kmod eliminates the TUN device overhead by processing packets directly inside the Linux network stack. It reuses rustguard-crypto and rustguard-core unchanged (both compiled as no_std) and replaces the TUN file descriptor with direct sk_buff / xdp_buff access via the kernel's netdev layer.
sections.content: rustguard-kmod eliminates the TUN device overhead by processing packets directly inside the Linux network stack. It reuses rustguard-crypto and rustguard-core unchanged (both compiled as no_std) and replaces the TUN file descriptor with direct sk_buff / xdp_buff access via the kernel's netdev layer.

sections.heading: Enrollment Mode
sections.embedText: rustguard-enroll adds a runtime control plane over rustguard-core. A UNIX domain socket accepts open, close, and status commands while the tunnel is live. Peer state (public keys, assigned IPs) is persisted to ~/.rustguard/state.json.
sections.content: rustguard-enroll adds a runtime control plane over rustguard-core. A UNIX domain socket accepts open, close, and status commands while the tunnel is live. Peer state (public keys, assigned IPs) is persisted to ~/.rustguard/state.json.

sections.heading: Cryptographic primitive layer for low-level operations
sections.embedText: The cryptographic primitive layer. Contains no protocol logic. All functions operate on byte slices; no heap allocation in no_std mode. Key types: X25519SecretKey, X25519PublicKey, ChaCha20Poly1305, Blake2sHmac, Hkdf, Tai64n.
sections.content: The cryptographic primitive layer. Contains no protocol logic. All functions operate on byte slices; no heap allocation in no_std mode. Key types: X25519SecretKey, X25519PublicKey, ChaCha20Poly1305, Blake2sHmac, Hkdf, Tai64n.

sections.heading: Protocol state machine implementing WireGuard handshake
sections.embedText: Protocol state machine implementing the WireGuard Noise_IKpsk2 handshake and transport session lifecycle. Handles the handshake message wire format (initiation, response, cookie reply), the 2048-bit sliding-window anti-replay bitmap, and the timer state machine (rekey, keepalive, session expiry). Produces and consumes opaque byte buffers; has no I/O dependency.
sections.content: Protocol state machine implementing the WireGuard Noise_IKpsk2 handshake and transport session lifecycle. Handles the handshake message wire format (initiation, response, cookie reply), the 2048-bit sliding-window anti-replay bitmap, and the timer state machine (rekey, keepalive, session expiry). Produces and consumes opaque byte buffers; has no I/O dependency.

sections.heading: Platform abstraction layer for TUN device access
sections.embedText:
Platform I/O layer. Abstracts TUN device creation and packet I/O behind a common trait. Implementations:

- **UtunDevice** — macOS utun via kernel control socket (AF_SYSTEM)
- **LinuxTunDevice** — Linux /dev/net/tun with IFF_TUN | IFF_NO_PI
- **MultiQueueTun** — Linux multi-queue TUN for parallel I/O
- **AfXdpSocket** — AF_XDP zero-copy path via UMEM ring buffers
- **IoUringTun** — io_uring-backed async TUN I/O
sections.content:
Platform I/O layer. Abstracts TUN device creation and packet I/O behind a common trait. Implementations:

- **UtunDevice** — macOS utun via kernel control socket (AF_SYSTEM)
- **LinuxTunDevice** — Linux /dev/net/tun with IFF_TUN | IFF_NO_PI
- **MultiQueueTun** — Linux multi-queue TUN for parallel I/O
- **AfXdpSocket** — AF_XDP zero-copy path via UMEM ring buffers
- **IoUringTun** — io_uring-backed async TUN I/O

sections.heading: Configuration management and tunnel operation
sections.embedText: Reads a wg.conf-compatible config file, configures peers and allowed IPs, then runs the tunnel loop. Handles IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack routing and OS route table management on both Linux and macOS. Provides clean shutdown with signal handling and route cleanup.
sections.content: Reads a wg.conf-compatible config file, configures peers and allowed IPs, then runs the tunnel loop. Handles IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack routing and OS route table management on both Linux and macOS. Provides clean shutdown with signal handling and route cleanup.

sections.heading: Enrollment protocol implementation with IP allocation
sections.embedText: Implements the enrollment protocol: a token-derived XChaCha20 key encrypts the initial key exchange, a CIDR-based IP pool allocator assigns peer addresses (server always gets .1), and a pairing window enforces physical-presence enrollment. State is serialized to JSON for restart persistence.

State is serialized to JSON for restart persistence.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.embedText: RustGuard implements the WireGuard protocol from scratch across seven Rust crates. Understanding the terminology used throughout the codebase requires familiarity with both the WireGuard whitepaper and the project-specific abstractions layered on top of it. This page defines each concept in the order a packet or connection encounters it.
sections.content:
RustGuard implements the WireGuard protocol from scratch across seven Rust crates. Understanding the terminology used throughout the codebase requires familiarity with both the WireGuard whitepaper and the project-specific abstractions layered on top of it. This page defines each concept in the order a packet or connection encounters it.

---

sections.heading: X25519 Elliptic-Curve Key Exchange
sections.embedText: Elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman key exchange over Curve25519. Used during the Noise_IK handshake to derive a shared secret from a static keypair and an ephemeral keypair. Each peer has a long-lived static X25519 keypair and generates a fresh ephemeral keypair for every handshake initiation.
sections.content: Elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman key exchange over Curve25519. Used during the Noise_IK handshake to derive a shared secret from a static keypair and an ephemeral keypair. Each peer has a long-lived static X25519 keypair and generates a fresh ephemeral keypair for every handshake initiation.

sections.heading: ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD Encryption
sections.embedText: Authenticated encryption with associated data (AEAD) used for all transport-layer packet encryption. Provides both confidentiality and integrity. The nonce is derived from the session counter; nonce exhaustion (at 2⁶⁰ messages) triggers a rekey rather than wrapping — encrypt() returns Option to signal exhaustion without panicking.
sections.content: Authenticated encryption with associated data (AEAD) used for all transport-layer packet encryption. Provides both confidentiality and integrity. The nonce is derived from the session counter; nonce exhaustion (at 2⁶⁰ messages) triggers a rekey rather than wrapping — encrypt() returns Option to signal exhaustion without panicking.

sections.heading: XChaCha20-Poly1305 for Cookie Protection
sections.embedText: An extended-nonce variant of ChaCha20-Poly1305. Used specifically for cookie encryption in the DoS-protection layer, where a 192-bit nonce eliminates the nonce-reuse risk present in the base construction.
sections.content: An extended-nonce variant of ChaCha20-Poly1305. Used specifically for cookie encryption in the DoS-protection layer, where a 192-bit nonce eliminates the nonce-reuse risk present in the base construction.

sections.heading: HMAC-BLAKE2s Pseudorandom Function
sections.embedText: RFC 2104 HMAC construction over BLAKE2s — the ipad/opad double-hash form. Used as the pseudorandom function (PRF) within WireGuard's HKDF-based key derivation. Note: keyed BLAKE2s alone is **not** equivalent and produces non-interoperable output.
sections.content: RFC 2104 HMAC construction over BLAKE2s — the ipad/opad double-hash form. Used as the pseudorandom function (PRF) within WireGuard's HKDF-based key derivation. Note: keyed BLAKE2s alone is **not** equivalent and produces non-interoperable output.

sections.heading: HKDF Key Derivation
sections.embedText: HMAC-based key derivation function. Chains HMAC-BLAKE2s calls to expand a shared secret into the chaining key, handshake hash, and session keys used for transport encryption.
sections.content: HMAC-based key derivation function. Chains HMAC-BLAKE2s calls to expand a shared secret into the chaining key, handshake hash, and session keys used for transport encryption.

sections.heading: TAI64N Timestamp Encoding
sections.embedText: International Atomic Time with nanosecond precision, encoded as a 12-byte big-endian value. Embedded in handshake initiation messages and validated on receipt to prevent timestamp replay attacks (stale initiations must be rejected).
sections.content: International Atomic Time with nanosecond precision, encoded as a 12-byte big-endian value. Embedded in handshake initiation messages and validated on receipt to prevent timestamp replay attacks (stale initiations must be rejected).

sections.heading: Noise_IKpsk2 Protocol Pattern
sections.embedText: The specific Noise Protocol Framework pattern used by WireGuard. I = the initiator's static key is transmitted (encrypted) during initiation. K = the responder's static key is pre-known to the initiator. psk2 = an optional pre-shared symmetric key is mixed in at pattern message 2. The handshake produces two session keys: one for each direction of transport.
sections.content: The specific Noise Protocol Framework pattern used by WireGuard. I = the initiator's static key is transmitted (encrypted) during initiation. K = the responder's static key is pre-known to the initiator. psk2 = an optional pre-shared symmetric key is mixed in at pattern message 2. The handshake produces two session keys: one for each direction of transport.

sections.heading: Chaining Key and Handshake Hash
sections.embedText: Two mutable state values evolved throughout the Noise handshake. The chaining key accumulates entropy from each DH operation; the handshake hash commits to the transcript. Both are zeroed on drop via ZeroizeOnDrop to limit key material lifetime in memory.
sections.content: Two mutable state values evolved throughout the Noise handshake. The chaining key accumulates entropy from each DH operation; the handshake hash commits to the transcript. Both are zeroed on drop via ZeroizeOnDrop to limit key material lifetime in memory.

sections.heading: Sender Index
sections.embedText: A 32-bit identifier chosen by the initiating peer to tag its handshake and associate subsequent transport packets with a session. Originally generated with a nanosecond clock hack; replaced with a CSPRNG (getrandom crate) to prevent predictability.
sections.content: A 32-bit identifier chosen by the initiating peer to tag its handshake and associate subsequent transport packets with a session. Originally generated with a nanosecond clock hack; replaced with a CSPRNG (getrandom crate) to prevent predictability.

sections.heading: Initial handshake authentication with static keys
sections.embedText: A 16-byte BLAKE2s MAC over the handshake message, keyed with the recipient's static public key. Always present. Verified **before** any DH operations to prevent CPU exhaustion from unauthenticated packets.
sections.content: A 16-byte BLAKE2s MAC over the handshake message, keyed with the recipient's static public key. Always present. Verified **before** any DH operations to prevent CPU exhaustion from unauthenticated packets.

sections.heading: Load-aware authentication with return-routability verification
sections.embedText: A 16-byte BLAKE2s MAC keyed with the current cookie value. Required only when the responder is under load. Proves the initiator can receive packets at the source IP address (return-routability check).
sections.content: A 16-byte BLAKE2s MAC keyed with the current cookie value. Required only when the responder is under load. Proves the initiator can receive packets at the source IP address (return-routability check).

sections.heading: Server-side cookie generation and validation
sections.embedText: Server-side component that maintains a rotating secret and generates/validates cookies. The rotating secret changes on a timer to limit cookie validity windows.
sections.content: Server-side component that maintains a rotating secret and generates/validates cookies. The rotating secret changes on a timer to limit cookie validity windows.

sections.heading: Client-side cookie storage and application
sections.embedText: Client-side component that stores the most recently received cookie and applies it as MAC2 in subsequent handshake initiations when prompted by the server.
sections.content: Client-side component that stores the most recently received cookie and applies it as MAC2 in subsequent handshake initiations when prompted by the server.

sections.heading: Bidirectional Encrypted Sessions
sections.embedText: A pair of ChaCha20-Poly1305 keys (send and receive) derived at the end of a successful Noise_IKpsk2 handshake. Each session has an independent nonce counter for each direction.
sections.content: A pair of ChaCha20-Poly1305 keys (send and receive) derived at the end of a successful Noise_IKpsk2 handshake. Each session has an independent nonce counter for each direction.

sections.heading: Replay Attack Prevention Mechanism
sections.embedText: A 2048-bit sliding window bitmap that tracks received packet counters. Protects against replay attacks. The window check and window update are deliberately split into check() and update() operations: the window is only advanced **after** AEAD verification succeeds, preventing garbage counters from advancing state.
sections.content: A 2048-bit sliding window bitmap that tracks received packet counters. Protects against replay attacks. The window check and window update are deliberately split into check() and update() operations: the window is only advanced **after** AEAD verification succeeds, preventing garbage counters from advancing state.

sections.heading: Automatic Session Rekeying
sections.embedText: Sessions are rekeyed after 120 seconds or 2⁶⁰ messages, whichever comes first. The timer state machine initiates a new Noise handshake; existing transport continues until the new session is established.
sections.content: Sessions are rekeyed after 120 seconds or 2⁶⁰ messages, whichever comes first. The timer state machine initiates a new Noise handshake; existing transport continues until the new session is established.

sections.heading: TUN Virtual Network Interface
sections.embedText:
A virtual network interface that exchanges raw IP packets with userspace. RustGuard reads plaintext IP packets from the TUN, encrypts them, and sends them over UDP; received UDP datagrams are decrypted and written back to TUN.

- **macOS**: utun device created via raw kernel control sockets.
- **Linux**: /dev/net/tun opened with IFF_TUN | IFF_NO_PI.
sections.content:
A virtual network interface that exchanges raw IP packets with userspace. RustGuard reads plaintext IP packets from the TUN, encrypts them, and sends them over UDP; received UDP datagrams are decrypted and written back to TUN.

- **macOS**: utun device created via raw kernel control sockets.
- **Linux**: /dev/net/tun opened with IFF_TUN | IFF_NO_PI.

sections.heading: Multi-Queue TUN Support
sections.embedText: Linux TUN supports multiple file-descriptor queues attached to a single interface, enabling parallel processing across CPU cores without a single-queue bottleneck.
sections.content: Linux TUN supports multiple file-descriptor queues attached to a single interface, enabling parallel processing across CPU cores without a single-queue bottleneck.

sections.heading: AF_XDP Kernel Bypass Sockets
sections.embedText: Linux kernel bypass socket family. Allows RustGuard to exchange packets directly between NIC hardware queues and userspace memory (UMEM), bypassing the kernel network stack entirely.
sections.content: Linux kernel bypass socket family. Allows RustGuard to exchange packets directly between NIC hardware queues and userspace memory (UMEM), bypassing the kernel network stack entirely.

sections.heading: io_uring Asynchronous I/O
sections.embedText: Linux asynchronous I/O interface. Used in rustguard-tun to issue TUN reads and UDP socket operations without blocking kernel transitions per packet.
sections.content: Linux asynchronous I/O interface. Used in rustguard-tun to issue TUN reads and UDP socket operations without blocking kernel transitions per packet.

sections.heading: AllowedIPs
sections.embedText: A per-peer set of IP prefixes (IPv4 and IPv6 CIDR ranges). Outbound packets are routed to the peer whose AllowedIPs set contains the destination address. Inbound decrypted packets are validated: if the source address does not match the peer's AllowedIPs, the packet is dropped. The parser handles both address families.
sections.content:
A per-peer set of IP prefixes (IPv4 and IPv6 CIDR ranges). Outbound packets are routed to the peer whose AllowedIPs set contains the destination address. Inbound decrypted packets are validated: if the source address does not match the peer's AllowedIPs, the packet is dropped. The parser handles both address families.

---

sections.heading: Pre-Shared Key (PSK)
sections.embedText: An optional 32-byte symmetric secret shared out-of-band between two peers. Mixed into the Noise handshake at step psk2. Provides a layer of post-quantum security: an attacker who later breaks X25519 cannot decrypt past sessions without also having the PSK. Implemented with backward-compatible wrappers so PSK-less peers interoperate.
sections.content:
An optional 32-byte symmetric secret shared out-of-band between two peers. Mixed into the Noise handshake at step `psk2`. Provides a layer of post-quantum security: an attacker who later breaks X25519 cannot decrypt past sessions without also having the PSK. Implemented with backward-compatible wrappers so PSK-less peers interoperate.

---

sections.heading: Enrollment Protocol
sections.embedText: A custom key-exchange protocol implemented in rustguard-enroll. A token-derived XChaCha20 key encrypts the key exchange, so no TLS or PKI is required. The server allocates an IP from the pool; the client receives its address and peer configuration in a single round trip.
sections.content: A custom key-exchange protocol implemented in rustguard-enroll. A token-derived XChaCha20 key encrypts the key exchange, so no TLS or PKI is required. The server allocates an IP from the pool; the client receives its address and peer configuration in a single round trip.

sections.heading: IP Pool
sections.embedText: A CIDR block (--pool 10.150.0.0/24) managed by the enrollment server. The server claims .1; each joining client receives the next unallocated address sequentially.
sections.content: A CIDR block (--pool 10.150.0.0/24) managed by the enrollment server. The server claims .1; each joining client receives the next unallocated address sequentially.

sections.heading: Pairing Window
sections.embedText: Enrollment is not always-open. A physical-presence model gates enrollment: the server starts with enrollment closed and requires an explicit rustguard open  command to accept new peers. The window auto-closes on expiry via an atomic timestamp. Modelled after Zigbee's pairing concept.
sections.content: Enrollment is not always-open. A physical-presence model gates enrollment: the server starts with enrollment closed and requires an explicit rustguard open  command to accept new peers. The window auto-closes on expiry via an atomic timestamp. Modelled after Zigbee's pairing concept.

sections.heading: Control Socket
sections.embedText: A UNIX domain socket used to send runtime management commands (open, close, status) to the running daemon without restarting the process.
sections.content: A UNIX domain socket used to send runtime management commands (open, close, status) to the running daemon without restarting the process.

sections.heading: Persistence
sections.embedText: Server state — peer public keys and their assigned IPs — is serialized to ~/.rustguard/state.json. The server reloads this state on restart; existing peers reconnect without re-enrolling.
sections.content: Server state — peer public keys and their assigned IPs — is serialized to ~/.rustguard/state.json. The server reloads this state on restart; existing peers reconnect without re-enrolling.

sections.heading: Kernel Module (`rustguard-kmod`)
sections.embedText: An out-of-tree Linux kernel module written in Rust with a C shim, targeting kernel 6.10+. Implements the WireGuard data path inside the kernel to eliminate the TUN overhead present in userspace — packets are encrypted and decrypted without a userspace round trip.
sections.content:
An out-of-tree Linux kernel module written in Rust with a C shim, targeting kernel 6.10+. Implements the WireGuard data path inside the kernel to eliminate the TUN overhead present in userspace — packets are encrypted and decrypted without a userspace round trip.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.embedText:
The following shows the lifecycle of a single outbound packet through RustGuard's conceptual layers:

The following shows a minimal rustguard-enroll token-based enrollment exchange:
sections.content:
The following shows the lifecycle of a single outbound packet through RustGuard's conceptual layers:

```
Plaintext IP packet
        │
        ▼
 AllowedIPs lookup ──► select peer
        │
        ▼
 Session lookup ──────► if no session: trigger Noise_IKpsk2 handshake
        │
        ▼
 ChaCha20-Poly1305 encrypt(nonce=counter, plaintext=packet)
        │
        ▼
 UDP send ────────────► remote endpoint
```

The following shows a minimal `rustguard-enroll` token-based enrollment exchange:

```bash
# Server: start with an IP pool, enrollment initially closed
rustguard serve --pool 10.150.0.0/24 --token mysecret

# Operator: open a 60-second enrollment window
rustguard open 60

# Client: join and receive an allocated IP
rustguard join 1.2.3.4:51820 --token mysecret

# Operator: inspect window state and peer count
rustguard status

# Operator: close enrollment immediately
rustguard close
```

```bash
# Traditional wg.conf mode still works; enrollment is additive
rustguard up wg0.conf
```

For a structural overview of the components these decisions produced, see [System Overview](01-System-Overview.md). For the concepts referenced here, see [Core Concepts](02-Core-Concepts.md).

sections.heading: Motivation for clean-room implementation
sections.embedText: Existing WireGuard userspace tools (wireguard-go, boringtun) wrap libwg or the official Go implementation. Using a binding would deliver a working tunnel faster but would make it impossible to deeply understand the protocol internals or push the implementation in directions the upstream library does not support.
sections.content: Existing WireGuard userspace tools (wireguard-go, boringtun) wrap libwg or the official Go implementation. Using a binding would deliver a working tunnel faster but would make it impossible to deeply understand the protocol internals or push the implementation in directions the upstream library does not support.

sections.heading: Clean-room implementation approach
sections.embedText: Implement every layer from scratch — cryptographic primitives, Noise handshake, wire format, TUN device management, timer state machine — with no dependency on any existing WireGuard library.
sections.content: Implement every layer from scratch — cryptographic primitives, Noise handshake, wire format, TUN device management, timer state machine — with no dependency on any existing WireGuard library.

sections.heading: Auditability and modifiability of protocol logic
sections.embedText: All protocol logic is auditable and modifiable within the repository.
sections.content: All protocol logic is auditable and modifiable within the repository.

sections.heading: Manual interoperability validation requirement
sections.embedText: Interoperability must be validated manually against the WireGuard specification rather than inherited from a reference library.
sections.content: Interoperability must be validated manually against the WireGuard specification rather than inherited from a reference library.

sections.heading: Bugs discovered and resolved through security audit
sections.embedText: Several bugs were introduced that a reference library would have prevented (see ADR-005 through ADR-010); all were found and fixed during the Phase 2 security audit.
sections.content: Several bugs were introduced that a reference library would have prevented (see ADR-005 through ADR-010); all were found and fixed during the Phase 2 security audit.

sections.heading: Shared Cryptography Requirement Between Kernel and Userspace
sections.embedText: Both the userspace daemon and the Linux kernel module (rustguard-kmod) require the same cryptographic primitives and Noise handshake logic. The kernel module cannot link against the Rust standard library.
sections.content: Both the userspace daemon and the Linux kernel module (rustguard-kmod) require the same cryptographic primitives and Noise handshake logic. The kernel module cannot link against the Rust standard library.

sections.heading: no_std-Compatible Crates with Optional std Feature
sections.embedText: rustguard-crypto and rustguard-core are written as no_std-compatible crates. All allocations are eliminated from their hot paths. The std feature flag re-enables convenience impls (e.g., std::error::Error) when building for userspace targets.
sections.content: rustguard-crypto and rustguard-core are written as no_std-compatible crates. All allocations are eliminated from their hot paths. The std feature flag re-enables convenience impls (e.g., std::error::Error) when building for userspace targets.

sections.heading: Canonical Crypto Implementation with Per-Module Constraints
sections.embedText: A single canonical implementation of X25519, ChaCha20-Poly1305, BLAKE2s, HKDF, TAI64N, and the Noise_IKpsk2 handshake is shared between both execution environments. rustguard-tun, rustguard-daemon, rustguard-enroll, and rustguard-cli are std-only and are not constrained by this decision.
sections.content: A single canonical implementation of X25519, ChaCha20-Poly1305, BLAKE2s, HKDF, TAI64N, and the Noise_IKpsk2 handshake is shared between both execution environments. rustguard-tun, rustguard-daemon, rustguard-enroll, and rustguard-cli are std-only and are not constrained by this decision.

sections.heading: WireGuard Noise_IKpsk2 Specification and Backward Compatibility
sections.embedText: WireGuard specifies Noise_IKpsk2 as its handshake pattern. The psk2 modifier places the pre-shared key (PSK) mix at the second message position, meaning PSK support is backward-compatible — a PSK of all-zeroes produces the standard Noise_IK flow.
sections.content: WireGuard specifies Noise_IKpsk2 as its handshake pattern. The psk2 modifier places the pre-shared key (PSK) mix at the second message position, meaning PSK support is backward-compatible — a PSK of all-zeroes produces the standard Noise_IK flow.

sections.heading: Implementation Strategy for Noise_IKpsk2
sections.embedText: Implement Noise_IKpsk2 exactly as specified in the WireGuard whitepaper. PSK wrappers expose a unified API: callers that do not configure a PSK pass the zero PSK implicitly.
sections.content: Implement Noise_IKpsk2 exactly as specified in the WireGuard whitepaper. PSK wrappers expose a unified API: callers that do not configure a PSK pass the zero PSK implicitly.

sections.heading: Interoperability and Compatibility Benefits
sections.embedText: Full interoperability with standard WireGuard peers that use a PSK. Peers without a PSK configured continue to work without code-path changes.
sections.content: Full interoperability with standard WireGuard peers that use a PSK. Peers without a PSK configured continue to work without code-path changes.

sections.heading: BLAKE2s Specification Incompatibility
sections.embedText: The initial implementation used BLAKE2s in keyed mode directly as a PRF. The WireGuard specification requires RFC 2104 HMAC construction over BLAKE2s (ipad/opad double-hash). These produce different output and are not interoperable.
sections.content: The initial implementation used BLAKE2s in keyed mode directly as a PRF. The WireGuard specification requires RFC 2104 HMAC construction over BLAKE2s (ipad/opad double-hash). These produce different output and are not interoperable.

sections.heading: RFC 2104 HMAC-BLAKE2s Adoption
sections.embedText: Replace keyed BLAKE2s with a correct RFC 2104 HMAC-BLAKE2s construction. The KDF chain is now interoperable with the reference WireGuard implementation.
sections.content: Replace keyed BLAKE2s with a correct RFC 2104 HMAC-BLAKE2s construction. The KDF chain is now interoperable with the reference WireGuard implementation.

sections.heading: Breaking Change and Correctness
sections.embedText: Breaking change at the wire level relative to early builds. All downstream key material (chaining keys, session keys) is now derived correctly.
sections.content: Breaking change at the wire level relative to early builds. All downstream key material (chaining keys, session keys) is now derived correctly.

sections.heading: Security vulnerability in packet processing order
sections.embedText: The original packet processing order performed the initiator's X25519 DH computation before verifying MAC1. An attacker could flood the server with unauthenticated initiation packets, consuming CPU on DH operations at negligible cost to the attacker.
sections.content: The original packet processing order performed the initiator's X25519 DH computation before verifying MAC1. An attacker could flood the server with unauthenticated initiation packets, consuming CPU on DH operations at negligible cost to the attacker.

sections.heading: MAC1 verification as first operation
sections.embedText: MAC1 is verified as the first operation on any received handshake packet. DH and subsequent operations are only reached if MAC1 is valid.
sections.content: MAC1 is verified as the first operation on any received handshake packet. DH and subsequent operations are only reached if MAC1 is valid.

sections.heading: Security and performance consequences
sections.embedText: Unauthenticated packets are rejected with a single HMAC-BLAKE2s call. Server-side DH is never performed for packets that fail MAC1.
sections.content: Unauthenticated packets are rejected with a single HMAC-BLAKE2s call. Server-side DH is never performed for packets that fail MAC1.

sections.heading: Original security vulnerability in replay protection
sections.embedText: The original replay protection implementation advanced the 2048-bit sliding window bitmap before verifying the AEAD tag. A network attacker could replay or corrupt packets with arbitrary counter values, poisoning the window and causing legitimate packets to be dropped.
sections.content: The original replay protection implementation advanced the 2048-bit sliding window bitmap before verifying the AEAD tag. A network attacker could replay or corrupt packets with arbitrary counter values, poisoning the window and causing legitimate packets to be dropped.

sections.heading: Two-phase replay window verification and update
sections.embedText: The replay window exposes two separate operations: check() tests whether a counter would be accepted (without mutating state), and update() advances the window (called only after successful AEAD verification).
sections.content: The replay window exposes two separate operations: check() tests whether a counter would be accepted (without mutating state), and update() advances the window (called only after successful AEAD verification).

sections.heading: Security and maintainability benefits
sections.embedText: Replay window state is never modified by a packet that fails authentication. The split API makes the correct ordering impossible to accidentally invert in future call sites.
sections.content: Replay window state is never modified by a packet that fails authentication. The split API makes the correct ordering impossible to accidentally invert in future call sites.

sections.heading: Problems with Hand-Rolled Constant-Time Comparisons
sections.embedText: Hand-rolled constant-time comparison functions using std::hint::black_box to suppress LLVM optimizations are fragile. Compiler behaviour is not specified and optimizations can change across Rust releases.
sections.content: Hand-rolled constant-time comparison functions using std::hint::black_box to suppress LLVM optimizations are fragile. Compiler behaviour is not specified and optimizations can change across Rust releases.

sections.heading: Adopting subtle::ConstantTimeEq for Secure Comparisons
sections.embedText: Replace all hand-rolled constant-time comparisons with subtle::ConstantTimeEq. The subtle crate is the de facto standard in the Rust cryptography ecosystem and provides compiler-portable guarantees.
sections.content: Replace all hand-rolled constant-time comparisons with subtle::ConstantTimeEq. The subtle crate is the de facto standard in the Rust cryptography ecosystem and provides compiler-portable guarantees.

sections.heading: Security and Maintenance Benefits
sections.embedText: Timing side-channel risk is delegated to a well-reviewed dependency. Internal implementations are removed; maintenance burden is reduced.
sections.content: Timing side-channel risk is delegated to a well-reviewed dependency. Internal implementations are removed; maintenance burden is reduced.

sections.heading: Portability and entropy-readiness issues with direct /dev/urandom reads
sections.embedText: Direct reads from /dev/urandom via the file descriptor API bypass the operating system's entropy-readiness guarantees on some Linux configurations (early boot, containers) and are not portable to non-Linux targets.
sections.content: Direct reads from /dev/urandom via the file descriptor API bypass the operating system's entropy-readiness guarantees on some Linux configurations (early boot, containers) and are not portable to non-Linux targets.

sections.heading: Migration to getrandom for cross-platform entropy management
sections.embedText: Replace all direct /dev/urandom reads with the getrandom crate, which calls the getrandom(2) syscall on Linux and the appropriate OS-native API on other platforms.
sections.content: Replace all direct /dev/urandom reads with the getrandom crate, which calls the getrandom(2) syscall on Linux and the appropriate OS-native API on other platforms.

sections.heading: Impact of getrandom adoption on entropy handling
sections.embedText:
Entropy reads block until the kernel's random pool is initialised.
CSPRNG seeding is correct on all supported platforms.
sections.content:
Entropy reads block until the kernel's random pool is initialised.
CSPRNG seeding is correct on all supported platforms.

sections.heading: Sensitive Key Material Persistence Risk
sections.embedText: Handshake state (chaining key, running hash, PSK) is held in heap or stack memory. Without explicit zeroing, sensitive key material persists in memory after the handshake completes or fails, where it could be recovered from process memory dumps or swap.
sections.content: Handshake state (chaining key, running hash, PSK) is held in heap or stack memory. Without explicit zeroing, sensitive key material persists in memory after the handshake completes or fails, where it could be recovered from process memory dumps or swap.

sections.heading: ZeroizeOnDrop Memory Zeroing Implementation
sections.embedText: All handshake state structs derive or implement ZeroizeOnDrop. Key material is overwritten with zeros when the struct is dropped, whether the handshake succeeded or not.
sections.content: All handshake state structs derive or implement ZeroizeOnDrop. Key material is overwritten with zeros when the struct is dropped, whether the handshake succeeded or not.

sections.heading: Memory-Level Security Benefits
sections.embedText: Forward secrecy properties hold at the memory level as well as the protocol level. Session keys and ephemeral private keys are not recoverable from a post-session memory snapshot.
sections.content: Forward secrecy properties hold at the memory level as well as the protocol level. Session keys and ephemeral private keys are not recoverable from a post-session memory snapshot.

sections.heading: Nonce exhaustion vulnerability and original failure mode
sections.embedText: ChaCha20-Poly1305 supports a 64-bit nonce counter. Exhausting the nonce space (2^64 messages) with the same session key would enable an attacker to observe key-stream reuse. The original implementation panicked on nonce overflow, which would crash the daemon in production.
sections.content: ChaCha20-Poly1305 supports a 64-bit nonce counter. Exhausting the nonce space (2^64 messages) with the same session key would enable an attacker to observe key-stream reuse. The original implementation panicked on nonce overflow, which would crash the daemon in production.

sections.heading: Solution: Option-based nonce overflow handling
sections.embedText: encrypt() returns Option and returns None when the nonce counter would overflow. The transport session layer treats None as a signal to force a rekey before any further encryption.
sections.content: encrypt() returns Option and returns None when the nonce counter would overflow. The transport session layer treats None as a signal to force a rekey before any further encryption.

sections.heading: Effects of enforcing nonce overflow as rekey trigger
sections.embedText: No nonce reuse is possible at the encryption API boundary. The daemon does not crash; it initiates a new handshake instead. The WireGuard timer-driven rekey (after 2^60 messages) remains the primary guard; Option is a last-resort safety net.
sections.content: No nonce reuse is possible at the encryption API boundary. The daemon does not crash; it initiates a new handshake instead. The WireGuard timer-driven rekey (after 2^60 messages) remains the primary guard; Option is a last-resort safety net.

sections.heading: Problem Statement: Nonce Size Limitation
sections.embedText: WireGuard's cookie reply message must be encrypted with a key derived from a server-side rotating secret and the initiator's source IP/port. A 24-byte nonce is desirable to allow random nonce generation without collision risk. ChaCha20-Poly1305 uses a 12-byte nonce, which is too short for random generation.
sections.content: WireGuard's cookie reply message must be encrypted with a key derived from a server-side rotating secret and the initiator's source IP/port. A 24-byte nonce is desirable to allow random nonce generation without collision risk. ChaCha20-Poly1305 uses a 12-byte nonce, which is too short for random generation.

sections.heading: Selected Solution: XChaCha20-Poly1305 Implementation
sections.embedText: Cookie reply encryption uses XChaCha20-Poly1305, which extends the nonce to 24 bytes using the HChaCha20 sub-key derivation. Nonces for cookie replies are generated from the CSPRNG.
sections.content: Cookie reply encryption uses XChaCha20-Poly1305, which extends the nonce to 24 bytes using the HChaCha20 sub-key derivation. Nonces for cookie replies are generated from the CSPRNG.

sections.heading: Security and Implementation Outcomes
sections.embedText: Random nonce generation is safe for cookie replies (collision probability negligible under 2^80 operations). The encryption used for cookie replies is distinct from transport encryption; the distinction is documented in code comments.
sections.content: Random nonce generation is safe for cookie replies (collision probability negligible under 2^80 operations). The encryption used for cookie replies is distinct from transport encryption; the distinction is documented in code comments.

sections.heading: Vulnerabilities in timestamp-based sender indices
sections.embedText: The initial implementation used the nanosecond timestamp as the 32-bit sender index in handshake messages. Timestamps are not unique under concurrent handshakes and are predictable, enabling index guessing attacks.
sections.content: The initial implementation used the nanosecond timestamp as the 32-bit sender index in handshake messages. Timestamps are not unique under concurrent handshakes and are predictable, enabling index guessing attacks.

sections.heading: CSPRNG-based sender index generation
sections.embedText: Sender indices are drawn from the operating system CSPRNG (via the getrandom crate). Collision probability for 32-bit random values across active sessions is negligible for all realistic peer counts.
sections.content: Sender indices are drawn from the operating system CSPRNG (via the getrandom crate). Collision probability for 32-bit random values across active sessions is negligible for all realistic peer counts.

sections.heading: Security and operational benefits
sections.embedText:
- Sender indices are unpredictable to off-path attackers.
- No coordination or global counter is required.
sections.content:
- Sender indices are unpredictable to off-path attackers.
- No coordination or global counter is required.

sections.heading: Secure Key Exchange Without Configuration Overhead
sections.embedText: The zero-config enrollment protocol (rustguard serve / rustguard join) needs to protect the key exchange between server and joining client. Using a separate PKI or TLS certificate would reintroduce manual configuration.
sections.content: The zero-config enrollment protocol (rustguard serve / rustguard join) needs to protect the key exchange between server and joining client. Using a separate PKI or TLS certificate would reintroduce manual configuration.

sections.heading: Token-Derived Encryption Key Scheme
sections.embedText: A shared --token string is used by both sides. An XChaCha20-Poly1305 key is derived from the token using HKDF-BLAKE2s. All enrollment messages are encrypted under this key. The token is a human-memorable secret (e.g., a passphrase) shared out-of-band.
sections.content: A shared --token string is used by both sides. An XChaCha20-Poly1305 key is derived from the token using HKDF-BLAKE2s. All enrollment messages are encrypted under this key. The token is a human-memorable secret (e.g., a passphrase) shared out-of-band.

sections.heading: Security Trade-offs and Implementation Implications
sections.embedText: No certificates or key files are required for enrollment. Security is bounded by the entropy of the token; short or reused tokens weaken the scheme. The token key is only used during enrollment; it is discarded once the WireGuard session key is established.
sections.content: No certificates or key files are required for enrollment. Security is bounded by the entropy of the token; short or reused tokens weaken the scheme. The token key is only used during enrollment; it is discarded once the WireGuard session key is established.

sections.heading: Security Risk of Persistent Enrollment
sections.embedText: Leaving enrollment permanently open means any party that knows the token can join at any time, including after the initial setup period. A Zigbee-style physical-presence model — open for a bounded time window, then closed — limits exposure.
sections.content: Leaving enrollment permanently open means any party that knows the token can join at any time, including after the initial setup period. A Zigbee-style physical-presence model — open for a bounded time window, then closed — limits exposure.

sections.heading: UNIX Socket-Based Time-Bounded Enrollment Implementation
sections.embedText: The server exposes a UNIX domain socket. Enrollment is closed by default. rustguard open opens the window for a bounded duration; rustguard close closes it immediately. The window state is tracked via an atomic timestamp. Existing peer sessions are unaffected by window state.
sections.content: The server exposes a UNIX domain socket. Enrollment is closed by default. rustguard open opens the window for a bounded duration; rustguard close closes it immediately. The window state is tracked via an atomic timestamp. Existing peer sessions are unaffected by window state.

sections.heading: Access Control and Risk Mitigation Benefits
sections.embedText: Enrollment is only possible during a window explicitly opened by an operator with local access to the server. The attack surface for token-based enrollment is time-limited. The UNIX socket is only accessible to processes with filesystem permission on the socket path (i.e., local users).
sections.content: Enrollment is only possible during a window explicitly opened by an operator with local access to the server. The attack surface for token-based enrollment is time-limited. The UNIX socket is only accessible to processes with filesystem permission on the socket path (i.e., local users).

sections.heading: Kernel-Userspace Boundary Performance Bottleneck
sections.embedText: Userspace WireGuard requires every packet to cross the kernel–userspace boundary twice (TUN read + UDP write for egress; UDP read + TUN write for ingress). At high throughput this boundary becomes the dominant cost. AF_XDP reduces copies but does not eliminate the boundary crossings entirely.
sections.content: Userspace WireGuard requires every packet to cross the kernel–userspace boundary twice (TUN read + UDP write for egress; UDP read + TUN write for ingress). At high throughput this boundary becomes the dominant cost. AF_XDP reduces copies but does not eliminate the boundary crossings entirely.

sections.heading: In-Kernel Module Implementation Strategy
sections.embedText: rustguard-kmod implements the complete data path inside the Linux kernel as an out-of-tree module targeting kernel 6.10+. The cryptographic and protocol logic is compiled from rustguard-crypto and rustguard-core (both no_std) directly into the module. No TUN device is involved.
sections.content: rustguard-kmod implements the complete data path inside the Linux kernel as an out-of-tree module targeting kernel 6.10+. The cryptographic and protocol logic is compiled from rustguard-crypto and rustguard-core (both no_std) directly into the module. No TUN device is involved.

sections.heading: Implementation Consequences and Technical Requirements
sections.embedText:
- The kernel–userspace boundary is eliminated for the data path; packets are encrypted/decrypted in-kernel.
- The module is out-of-tree and requires kernel headers for the target kernel version to build.
- The minimum supported kernel is 6.10 (required for stable Rust-in-kernel ABI support).


### Enrollment window lifecycle

```bash
# Open enrollment for 60 seconds (local operator action)
rustguard open 60

# Remote client joins during the window
rustguard join 198.51.100.1:51820 --token mysecret

# Close the window immediately after
rustguard close
```

### Checking replay window split usage (illustrative)

```typescript
// Pseudocode reflecting the check/update split enforced by ADR-006
const counter = packet.counter;
if (!replayWindow.check(counter)) {
  return drop(packet); // reject before AEAD
}
const plaintext = aead.open(packet.ciphertext, packet.tag);
if (plaintext === null) {
  return drop(packet); // AEAD failed — do NOT call update()
}
replayWindow.update(counter); // advance window only after authentication
```

sections.heading: Overview
sections.embedText:
RustGuard exposes two distinct API surfaces:

- **CLI** (rustguard-cli): The primary user-facing interface for managing tunnels, enrollment, and key generation.
- **Library crates**: Programmatic Rust APIs for cryptography (rustguard-crypto), protocol state (rustguard-core), TUN device management (rustguard-tun), and zero-config enrollment (rustguard-enroll).

All library crates support dual std/no_std compilation unless noted otherwise.

RustGuard exposes two distinct API surfaces:

- **CLI** (`rustguard-cli`): The primary user-facing interface for managing tunnels, enrollment, and key generation.
- **Library crates**: Programmatic Rust APIs for cryptography (`rustguard-crypto`), protocol state (`rustguard-core`), TUN device management (`rustguard-tun`), and zero-config enrollment (`rustguard-enroll`).

All library crates support dual `std`/`no_std` compilation unless noted otherwise.

sections.heading: Starting a WireGuard Tunnel
sections.embedText:
### rustguard up

Starts a WireGuard tunnel from a standard wg.conf-formatted configuration file.

Reads peer definitions, assigns AllowedIPs routes (IPv4 and IPv6 CIDR), configures a TUN interface, and enters the tunnel loop. Handles SIGTERM/SIGINT for clean shutdown with route cleanup.

sections.heading: Running a WireGuard Server
sections.embedText:
### rustguard serve

Starts a WireGuard server with the zero-config enrollment protocol. Enrollment begins **closed**; use [rustguard open](#rustguard-open) to admit clients.

Server state (peer keys, assigned IPs) is persisted to ~/.rustguard/state.json and survives restarts.
sections.content:
### rustguard serve

Starts a WireGuard server with the zero-config enrollment protocol. Enrollment begins **closed**; use [rustguard open](#rustguard-open) to admit clients.

Server state (peer keys, assigned IPs) is persisted to ~/.rustguard/state.json and survives restarts.

sections.heading: Enrolling a Client in a Running Server
sections.embedText:
### rustguard join

Enrolls a client into a running rustguard serve instance. Generates a local keypair, performs the token-encrypted key exchange, and receives an assigned IP from the server's pool.

No configuration files are written or required. The tunnel starts immediately after successful enrollment.
sections.content:
### rustguard join

Enrolls a client into a running rustguard serve instance. Generates a local keypair, performs the token-encrypted key exchange, and receives an assigned IP from the server's pool.

No configuration files are written or required. The tunnel starts immediately after successful enrollment.

sections.heading: Managing Server Enrollment Window
sections.embedText:
### rustguard open

Opens the enrollment window on a running server for a specified number of seconds. Uses the UNIX domain control socket established by rustguard serve.

The window closes automatically via an atomic timestamp expiry. Existing peers are unaffected.

### rustguard close

Closes the enrollment window immediately on a running server.

No arguments. Communicates with the server via UNIX domain control socket.

sections.heading: Noise protocol implementation and session management (rustguard-core)
sections.embedText:
### rustguard-core

Implements the Noise_IKpsk2 handshake, transport sessions, replay window, and timer state machine. Dual std/no_std.

#### Replay Window

The replay window is a 2048-bit sliding bitmap. Verification and state mutation are separated to prevent window poisoning by unauthenticated packets.

#### Handshake State

Handshake chaining key, hash, and PSK are ZeroizeOnDrop — secret material is cleared from memory when the struct is dropped.

#### Cookie Mechanism (DoS Protection)

MAC1 is verified before any DH operations are performed. MAC2 is required when the server is under load.
